metrics: Hiring across departments (engineering, ops, others). Currently paying $400-$500 per LinkedIn job post with no tracking on ROI. Need to centralize candidate data. Planning to hire a recruiter.

decision_criteria: Price is the #1 factor - "Probably the price" when asked about deal-breakers. Whoever offers the best mix of functionality at the cheapest price wins. Centralized notes and candidate history. Career page.
decision_process: Faye evaluates multiple vendors through SSR → compares pricing and features → decision. She's out for 2 weeks in June, so timeline pushes to end of June / early July.
pain: No ATS at all - everything is manual. Only 1 person can screen candidates on LinkedIn. Sharing resumes via Slack with no tracking. Manual scheduling via Google Calendar. No interview kits or structured evaluation. No analytics or hiring data. Paying per LinkedIn post with no ROI visibility. "Would you mind just send me those emails? So I can just see." - basic level of evaluation suggests early-stage buyer.
champion: Faye Zheng - early-stage champion. Interested but not deeply engaged in the demo (kept cutting to pricing questions, wanted to skip workflow details). Lower energy/engagement compared to other prospects.
competition: Other SSR-recommended vendors (unnamed, "Yes, exactly" when asked if evaluating others). LinkedIn status quo.
compelling_event: End-of-summer / Q3 project per SSR notes. No hard deadline. Faye is out for 2 weeks in June, pushing realistic evaluation to July. No forcing function identified.

mru: 7/7 - Demo w/ team was good.  They like the tool and see how it can eliminate multiple systems and consolidate into one, exploratory rn connecting later in year
metrics:
Current Headcount: Max of 150 employees (they do not anticipate going over this).

Time to hire: Currently averaging under 90 days.
economic_buyer: Frances
decision_criteria:
Must be an "all-in-one" solution to reduce their reliance on outside tools (like Guide and Bright Hire).

Needs to drastically improve scheduling efficiency (especially across time zones) and handle group/panel interviews without breaking.

Must provide a clean, consolidated UI for recruiters so they don't have to "click around and search" just to see their jobs.

Must maintain strict candidate privacy/bias controls (e.g., hiding screening notes from downstream stakeholders).

Requires strong API integrations (currently using Rippling for HRIS, JuiceBox for sourcing, and VerifiedFirst for background checks).
decision_process:
They are currently doing an exploratory "tour of new ones" to see what is on the market.

They plan to narrow down their shortlist and schedule more formal/custom demos over the summer.

A final decision and migration will happen ahead of their February 2027 Greenhouse renewal.
pain:
Disjointed Tech Stack: They are relying heavily on third-party tools (Guide for scheduling/candidate experience, Bright Hire for interview recording) because Greenhouse's native functionality is lacking.

Scheduling Nightmares: Greenhouse has severe limitations with availability requests and group scheduling. They are experiencing issues where candidates cancel last minute across time zones, and the system fails to notify the hiring managers properly.

Clunky UI: Jennifer specifically called out that the Greenhouse dashboard is unhelpful and doesn't contribute to productivity because it only displays three jobs at a time.

Manual Resume Review: Greenhouse's resume review capabilities are not as advanced as they need, forcing them to do highly manual, thorough recruiter screens to maintain their "high standard."
champion: Zeid and Jennifer likely bigggest users so them
competition:
Greenhouse (Incumbent).

Lever (Jennifer previously trialed them but found them too similar to Greenhouse).

+ many others they are open book rn

mru: 7/7 - Second call with Formic (Megan Bosch + Theresa Dobson). This is a sophisticated buyer - Theresa has 15 years of recruiting experience, was an early Greenhouse customer, and led global Greenhouse implementations at BCG.
metrics: 3-6 hires/month. High application volumes (3,000 on a Chief of Staff role; 150-250 for niche robotics roles). Time to hire reduction is a key metric. Interview prep time: their CRO went from 30 min to 5 min with Copilot.
economic_buyer: Theresa Dobson - VP/Head of Talent. Has deep ATS expertise (15 years, early Greenhouse customer, led BCG global implementations). She is the clear decision-maker on tooling.
decision_criteria: (1) AI screening & candidate surfacing - must match/exceed their custom-built internal tool (built by VP of Software using Greenhouse API + Claude/ChatGPT). (2) MCP capabilities (big deal - Theresa compared to Greenhouse MCP). (3) Stakeholder management / exec briefings - ability to auto-generate executive summaries for CEO interviews. (4) Sourcing (LinkedIn Recruiter integration - parity expected). (5) Talent pool search (location-based, qualitative AI search). (6) Data transfer integrity from Greenhouse. (7) Reporting & analytics.
decision_process: Theresa + Megan evaluating. Theresa is highly skeptical and analytical. Wants: (1) customer reference from comparable company (Series B/C startup, US-based, with talent leader). (2) MCP demo. (3) Reporting/analytics walkthrough (15 min follow-up). (4) Pricing/timing email. They will also check if Greenhouse has MCP and compare "apples to apples."
paper_process: Annual agreement. Split payment discussed and Tyler offered flexibility. Data transfer from Greenhouse is a concern - worst case fields import as notes.
pain: Greenhouse UI/UX has degraded - "clunkier," what used to be one click is now multiple. Limited native AI - they had to build a custom AI sourcing tool on top of Greenhouse using Claude/ChatGPT API. Manual executive briefing prep is time-consuming. Searching within Greenhouse is filter-based, not AI-intuitive. Location field in Greenhouse is inconsistent/weird.
champion: Megan Bosch - she's the daily user and is clearly more receptive to switching. She drove the first call and the follow-up. However, Theresa is the gatekeeper - skeptical, deeply experienced, asking hard questions. Megan is the champion; Theresa is the economic buyer who needs to be convinced.
competition: Greenhouse (incumbent, $8,800/yr at lower headcount). Tyler mentioned Greenhouse and Ashby as main competitors. Greenhouse MCP is being evaluated for comparison. Greenhouse renewal quote pending.
compelling_event: Greenhouse renewal coming up (renewal quote pending). Tyler recommended signing in July to allow implementation buffer. Offered 3 free months to bridge overlap.

(3) User experience - Paycom's UX is "straight garbage." (4) Automation to reduce admin burden. (5) Candidate sharing UX (vs. Paycom's broken email notifications). (6) Integration with Lumber (new HRIS). (7) Adobe Sign integration. (8) Rec approval + job offer approval workflows. (9) Customer-fed product development.
decision_process: Complex. Lumber transition is already in motion - Dan joined after wheels were turning. TT would likely be a bolt-on/add-on if Lumber's ATS is insufficient. No internal marketing department for employer branding.
champion: Dan - highly engaged, technically savvy, understands the ATS space well. He's the first dedicated recruiter and "the number one user of the system." next_steps:

mru: 7/7 - First 30-minute intro/demo call completed. Tyler showed career site, AI screening criteria, Ask Copilot, interview recording/transcription, and video platform.
metrics: Not specifically quantified; team of at least 3 (Leslie, Lauren, Jubel); using 5+ separate tools today
economic_buyer: CEO - "our CEO is very bought into finding the right tools to help... be more efficient" (Lauren). Leslie appears to be the HR decision-maker
decision_criteria: (1) Consolidate 5+ tools into one platform; (2) AI that actually works (powered by real LLM, not in-house); (3) Talent identification through hiring lifecycle; (4) Interview recording/transcription to replace MetaView; (5) Deepfake/fraud detection; (6) Accuracy & reliability of AI notetaker; (7) Candidate opt-out for recording
decision_process: Leslie/Lauren/Jubel internal discussion → circle back with Tyler to indicate interest → likely second deeper demo
paper_process: Workable contract runs through end of March; Tyler offered to help avoid unwanted renewal
pain: "Instead of having 5+ different tools, we would have one platform that did all of those things for us as well or better"; Workable AI doesn't work - "I feel like it's just not working for what we need" (Lauren); Workable AI is not real AI - "their AI is not really AI. They call it AI. It is an in-house developed tool" (Tyler); Want Phenom-like talent platform but Phenom won't talk to them - "Phenom won't even talk to us, literally will not even talk to us. We're too small"
champion: Lauren Schwarz - initially engaged with Tyler, brought in Leslie; "You hit us at the right time... because we're exploring things"; Leslie Hafter - senior, asking detailed questions about reliability, fraud detection, company history
competition: Workable (incumbent); Phenom (aspirational but won't engage); LinkedIn Hiring Assistant (1 seat, exploring)
compelling_event: Workable contract ends March; CEO mandate to find AI-driven tools; Lauren tested Workable AI agent day before call and it failed

next_steps:
Who: All
What: Reconnect for hands-on follow-up demo
When: ~1-2 weeks out (after July 4th)
mru: 7/7 - Intro call with Megan Scher of Jambalaya Group. They're a consulting firm recruiting on behalf of ~150 Ameriprise Financial practices and are unhappy with Hireology.
metrics: 10-30 open jobs at any time across ~22 active practices. 150 total client practices. Volume is low-to-moderate per role but spikes on admin/client service roles.
economic_buyer: Unknown. Margaret may be a decision-maker or co-evaluator (wasn't on call). No budget authority identified.
decision_criteria: Multi-entity/division posting under client brands (not Jambalaya Group); AI resume screening; ease of use; reporting/analytics; Teams integration; skills testing (Word/Excel/Outlook); DiSC profiles; Salesforce integration (not available).
decision_process: Megan + Margaret evaluating. Trial first → follow-up demo in ~1-2 weeks. No formal process articulated.
pain: Currently on Hireology - "we don't love it." Manual resume screening. Need multi-brand posting (recruiting on behalf of Ameriprise practices, not under Jambalaya Group name). Want AI to help sift through high-volume admin roles. Use Teams transcription for notes - want to keep or improve that.
champion: Megan Scher - actively driving the evaluation, asked for trial, engaged throughout. Evidence: requested demo, asked for trial same day, proposed follow-up meeting.
competition: Hireology (incumbent). No other vendors mentioned.
compelling_event: None explicitly stated. Organic dissatisfaction with Hireology.

next_steps: TBD She has to re engage but liked what she saw

champion: Tania Colon
competition: Status quo

mru:

metrics: The discussion included the potential pricing structure, which is around $18,000 annually for Nicholas and Company Inc. for Teamtailor's services. This indicates a focus on cost-effectiveness in relation to their current spending with ADP.
economic_buyer: Jenna Dunaske, Kyli Adamson, and Sara Smith are involved in the discussion, with Kyli indicating that Gia, their VP, will make the final decision. This suggests that Gia is the economic buyer.
decision_criteria: The integration with ADP was a significant point of discussion, particularly the ease of transferring candidate data and the functionality of the onboarding process. The ability to create multiple offer letter templates was also highlighted as a decision factor.
decision_process: The team plans to have an internal meeting to discuss the potential switch to Teamtailor, with Tyler suggesting a follow-up meeting to address any questions or concerns.
pain: The conversation touched on the challenges with ADP, particularly regarding the integration and data transfer, indicating that there are pain points in their current system that Teamtailor aims to alleviate.
champion: Jenna Dunaske appears to be an advocate for Teamtailor, actively asking questions and seeking clarity on features and integrations, which indicates her support for the potential switch.

next_steps:
Who: Madelyn & Trevor
What: Complete ADP ATS demo (same day)
When: Day of Call 2
mru: 7/7 - Two calls completed - full platform demo is done. Call 1 covered career site, connect/talent pool, AI screening, Smart Moves automation, candidate card, Ask Copilot, scheduling, interview recording, and offer letters.
metrics: 170 employees; paying $10K/yr for Jobvite; ~10,000 candidates in talent pool; hire for multi-location offices; ~75% of hires from self-sourcing; majority of roles require professional engineering license
economic_buyer: Caitlin (HR Director) - "It's more so like a Caitlin decision for sure" (Trevor on budget); she's used SSR before at prior company; hasn't been on either call yet
decision_criteria: (1) Reporting - new hire reports, application source tracking, automated scheduled reports; (2) Filtering/search within candidate pool (e.g., Big Four companies, universities, PE license); (3) Offer letter templates per role type; (4) ADP integration (push candidate data at hired stage); (5) AI bias safeguards; (6) LinkedIn Recruiter integration; (7) User-friendly drag-and-drop interface; (8) Custom fields & tags for engineering licenses
decision_process: Trevor & Madelyn evaluate → demo ADP ATS (same day as Call 2) + 1-2 more demos → narrow to ~3 finalists → bring Caitlin for final evaluation → decision by end of year → live by ~February
paper_process: Budget ~$10K/yr; decision by ~December; go-live target ~February; candidate data migration (10K candidates + resumes) needed - 1-2 month process; Tyler recommended signing Oct/Nov to allow setup time
pain: "Jobvite... it's really hard to get [things] fixed in a timely manner" (Madelyn on support); "There's so many different and unique automations... but they are not up to speed on that" (Madelyn on Jobvite automation gaps); Finance position had 400 applicants - CFO wanted to filter by Big Four companies, couldn't do it - "tap, tap, tap, tap, tap, tap... just trying to look for these Big Four companies"; Reporting is "very finicky" and required Trevor to do heavy setup; No rating system - only hashtag tagging; Jobvite doesn't currently push candidates to ADP
champion: Madelyn Garrett - highly engaged across both calls, asked probing questions about implementation/support, AI bias, LinkedIn integration, PE licensing custom fields; transparent about process; "I definitely think this one is... definitely built for recruiters"
competition: Jobvite (incumbent - "probably not going to use"); Greenhouse (being evaluated); BambooHR ATS (being evaluated); iCIMS (Caitlin has looked at before); ADP Recruiting Module (demoing same day as Call 2 - "biggest sell of ADP right now... having everything on one platform"); 1-2 others TBD
compelling_event: Decision by end of year; go-live target February; ADP demo happening same day as TT Call 2

next_steps: She needs to find out when their contract is up. Could either be EOY or next summer
metrics: Macey is focused on efficiency and consolidation. Key success markers include reducing the "Frankenstein" tech stack, improving hiring manager adoption, and streamlining candidate sourcing/ATS workflows.

decision_criteria: The prospect prioritizes employer branding (career site customization, team stories), ease of use (UI/UX), and automation (triggers, scheduling, AI screening). Integration capabilities with their existing stack (Rippling, Fetcher) are also critical.
pain: The current "Frankenstein" stack (Greenhouse + Fetcher + others) is clunky, causes friction between systems, and suffers from low hiring manager adoption.
champion: Macey Hallstedt
competition: Ashby and others
